item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thx, guys! Not really used to civilized debates on the internetz these days, but this has been really insightful. And I totally agree, it's really a pretty minor issue overall.

I also finally managed to get through the whole thing and the acronyms actually weren't that bad in the end. I actually really like the general tone of the skip, as there is a solid clinical tone to it, which is partly due to the heavy use of acronyms and it fits my head canon perfectly. Wholeheartedly +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's fine, none of this is coming off as dickish. sirpudding's mention of a volume indicating a 3-dimensional space (versus an area's 2-D space) is partly why I went with volume; keep in mind, this is the 'mathematic' definition of area (obviously, we refer to 3-dimensional spaces as 'areas' in less formal conversations all the time). Another reason I went with 'volume' is because it implies a space that is moving (it's linked to the movement of the boat).

The inconsistencies re: footnotes vs parenthesis boils down to me wrestling with an old bug. You might notice that every time I use a footnote here, I do so on the first line of a paragraph. This is because back when this article was written, Sigma-9 (the default theme) would displace any line containing a footnote (pushing the line slightly lower to make room for the super-script). This bugged the hell out of me visually. So, I worked around it by always making sure that when I used a footnote, it was on the first line. If I needed to explain an acronym on a line after the first, I'd use parenthesis.

The glitch has since been fixed — footnotes no longer displace the lines that contain them. But I've never gotten around to adjusting my older articles, because… well, to be quite frank, I don't really think it's that important.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't want to come across as a smart ass here, but isn't an area also 3D?

Mathematically, no, area is planar.

In this kind of usage, "area" usually implies a mostly ground level zone while "volume" conveys that a three dimensional space is definitely what's meant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't want to come across as a smart ass here, but isn't an area also 3D?

Off-topic: is this the right place for these kinds of discussions? I still feel like a super n00b and it's kind of intimidating to get into these things with people that have been around here much longer than I have.

I should also note that English is not actually my first language, so I don't have any problems yielding to native speakers; I actually consider these exchanges to be extremely insightful. If you guys say volume works fine here, then volume works fine!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This usage of"volume" is equivalent to the usage of area in "an area surrounding…". It's a volume and not an area because it's 3D.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I was going by this definition:

the amount of space that a substance or object occupies, or that is enclosed within a container.

I found another definition:

an amount or quantity of something, especially when great.

So you're probably technically correct, by that second definition. I'd still suggest considering to change it, because it just seems awkward to me. But that might be my personal preference.

Edit:
While I have you, I'm having a hard time getting through this article due to the multitude of acronyms. I'm hesitant to even mention this, since I haven't finished the article and I'd rather double check after I've finished it, but my first impression is that there are some inconsistencies regarding this issue. Some acronyms are explained in the footnotes, others in parenthesis. Either way, I find it difficult to keep their meaning in mind while I'm advancing further into the story. I'll try to get back to this once I've finished and explain better.

P. S. Hoping to not come across as a dick here. I think I am going to like this skip :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope! A volume is, in fact, a space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3241 is an irregularly shaped volume (approximately twelve kilometers in diameter) centered upon the SS Sommerfeld

Isn't "volume" the wrong word here? I suggest changing it to "space" (or maybe "area")
Congrats Hippo!

item.title: Re: Interesting.
item.content:encoded:
It's more analogous to claiming medicine heals wounds by enforcing the body's "natural" state. Medicine interacts with our biology; thinking this interaction is always a positive one (or represents a shift to "normalcy") is an extremely dangerous mindset.

Drugs are not magical things; they're not pills you take that increase your "health" stat. They're chemicals we use in very precise ways to disrupt our body's functions (in a way that hopefully leaves us healthier for it). Using them frivolously can have unforeseen consequences (see: strains of antibiotic-resistant bacteria).

SRAs are not magical things; they're not machines you use that increase an area's "normalcy" stat. They're devices we use in very precise ways to disrupt reality (in a way that hopefully leaves reality safer for it). Using them frivolously can have unforeseen consequences (see: SRAs start going nuclear).

item.title: Interesting.
item.content:encoded: I liked the concept of SRAs going to shit is a nice concept. However, I disagree with the first criticism: saying that SRAs are bad because they rewrite reality to fix rewritten reality as bad is analogous to saying medicine is bad because it uses harm (drugs that have bad side effects from chemicals to fix bad effects from chemicals, knife wound surgery is essentially metal penetration to fix metal penetration).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, man. I love me some body horror reality breakdowns, the Foundation (or assorted other GOI's) accidentally making anomalous clusterfucks like this, and terrible petty people getting their just deserts. This already had me good… and then the crosslinks at the very end happened.

Cue me gasping and a full ten seconds or so of whispering "oh shit" in tones of increasingly delighted horror. +1, time to go binge every Hippo article on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That always honestly bugged me, just never enough to fix. Thank you — added the code! I appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very minor comment, but the hr line break in the terminal at the end is grey, which I thought looked out of place with the rest of the green colors of the terminal. I cooked up some simple CSS to change it and make it the same color green as the quoteblock border.

 div.terminal hr { background-color: #80FF80; }

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She's got a pack of ice-cold beers she'll be sharing with Voccola. xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, is O5-5 going to laugh and make bets on how long it'll be before 106 gets tired of Dan's screams?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why's it look like wet Spaghetti-O's????? D: +1 fuck this shit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I based this off several real-world man-made catastrophes that could have been avoided (combined with my frustration over how the responsible parties always escape consequence — see the final email from the O5 after the footnotes), so I'm glad to hear it evoked a sense of realism!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this really might be among my favorite articles on the site. I love the way the story is conveyed through the quote blocks, and I love a disaster caused by human overconfidence. The idea that people could say "well, the drone broke with just one of these things operating on it, let's slap a few more of them on a boat, and send people in" is unfortunatly realistic. The line from the surviving agent, about how the machines just broke, rather than having the extreme consequences seen in the humans, evoked for me the infamous Thalidomide trials, where a little more testing also ought to have revealed the potential disaster before it affected real people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"4 metres per day" … oh hey, that doesn't seem like too mu—- mental maths happens

…that's 1.461km, or almost a mile, per year. O shi-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …is this how 106 ended up being called Scranton Oldman in the Man Zoo League/Overbobble Council canon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One good fictional analogy may be the containment vessel in Ghostbusters - shut it off suddenly and all hell breaks loose.

Though a better one may be the real-life example of Fukushima Daiichi….
+ show block
– hide block

Thanks to poor design and engineering, the tsunami essentially caused a haaaaaard shutdown of the plant, which was very bad news thanks to the outdated reactor design plus the co-sited waste storage pools. The main reactors were scrammed successfully, meaning a primary meltdown or super-critical explosion such as that suffered by Chernobyl couldn't happen, but it also meant that - with the site's backup generators being at sea level and so completely swamped, no substantial backup batteries, and the local grid having gone down - there was no power to the coolant circulation pumps, with no other backup system such as thermosyphoning available. And the fuel rods and moderator matrices were still pretty hot (in operation, there's something of a temperature gradient between the reactor and its coolant), and moreover they and the significant quantity of spent rods in the storage pools had a great deal of "decay heat" locked up in them that can take months to relax and release between their retirement from active service and the point at which they become safe to transport to long-term offsite storage.

So you kinda need those pumps to keep running, long after a scram, to keep the site safe. There is no instant "turning off" of a fission reactor that's been in productive service for some time, there's a protracted dwell time where it's still kicking out a lot of heat and secondary radiation (albeit not enough to sustain a chain reaction), which is why shutdowns for scheduled maintenance can take reactors out of circulation for many weeks and need careful timetabling.

A hard shutdown that takes the pumps offline almost immediately (if there were any backup batteries, they'd have drained within a few hours), as if you'd slammed a big red "shut down everything" button that physically cut all the cables, is a very bad idea because before too long you get coolant boiling in the pipes (or pools…), the steam becoming superheated and building up extreme pressure, exploding and blowing off the roof and cracking the reactor containment vessel, the pools boiling dry and exposing the red hot waste rods to the air (contamination and explosion risk), and ultimately causing a secondary meltdown of the scrammed fuel rods with the uranium breaking out of the rods and moderator and pooling on the floor of the reactor.

Plus there's a very good chance that the Godzilla contained within the vessel by the Japanese branch of the Foundation will escape,

and the whole district will have to be evacuated and cordoned off on the basis of supposed radioactive contamination to prevent civilian deaths and breaking of the masquerade…

tl;dr sudden stops are bad for nuclear reactors, modern computers, and fast-moving human beings, as well as probably for Scranton Reality Anchors.

(I suppose a simpler way of explaining it in-universe could be that suddenly collapsing the artificial morphological field could be like turning off the power to an electrical coil and collapsing the EM field surrounding it;
+ show block
– hide block

do that in a controlled fashion, through a transformer, and you have the basis of a car ignition system that produces hundreds of high powered sparks per second from a low voltage battery, and has somewhat deleterious effects on nearby radio equipment if not supplied with proper RF suppression as it's effectively a spark gap transmitter in the hundreds-of-watts range. Larger equivalents are used in research labs to create artificial lightning strikes, and it's the basis of experimental EMP weaponry.

Now think about what that might mean for the fabric of reality surrounding something which performs a similar trick with morphological fields… cut it off suddenly instead of bleeding it down in a controlled fashion and you get a huge pulse broadcast through the nearby Hume field… catastrophic levels of Kant flux… or maybe the other way round, IDK)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Updated the final dropdown to use the CSS wizardry for terminals on the SCP Style Resource page (options #8 and #5, produced by Dr Aers and 7happy7 respectively).

item.title: Re: Author Post (Updates!)
item.content:encoded:
I've made a few significant (though small) tweaks to the article:

  • The SS Sommerfeld was now constructed to transport low-risk anomalies and anesthesized ontokineticists (reality-benders). This is to better fit into the lore theme of Scranton Reality Anchors being a way of addressing reality-benders.
  • I modified the discussion throughout the article regarding SRAs to better reflect this lore.
  • I made the footnotes visible (mobile users reported having difficulty sometimes seeing them), and displaced them above the O5-5 note.
  • Changed the O5-5's passcode to something a little more literary (and, in my mind, far more menacing). Also tweaked a few bits of her note that always bugged the heck out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't want there to be many skips like this, but as what it is this is brilliantly executed. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not so unprecedented. Daniel DeVorn did SCP-3005. It's his fault!!

He indirectly caused Fifthism!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my god now that I just realized this, me too! One of my favorite -Js. If J skips are written by SCP employees, then maybe that's where it came from. (Or in-universe, instead of that article, it's literally a Scooby Doo reference.)

I guess the footnote could say where the terminology originated from, but I dunno, up to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. You know, it didn't even occur to me, but you're absolutely right. That's exactly what happened.

…

…

…

#MakeKeterDutyGreatAgain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was definitely my intent, yeah. The anomaly is the malfunctioning SRAs and how they've interacted with a boatload of low-risk anomalies, resulting in an area of reality that's effectively just "broken".

I put in the bit about the RSV Silent Spring to demonstrate just how fucked up this could get early on, but if I described all the anomalies going on inside of SCP-3241, I pretty much wouldn't know where to stop. It's basically a volume of space filled with real-life video-game glitches that are randomly changing at any given moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't actually think that's the case here though? My read is that the anomaly itself is due to the SRAs on the boat going critical (and possibly interacting with the other anomalies on board), which ties in rather nicely with the rest of the article. Going into more details about the anomaly itself could just… sort of go on forever, since it's effectively just a chunk of the world where reality is randomly broken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This felt like a whole tale series packaged in one SCP, and I enjoyed the hell out of it. The way that the site’s writing conventions have changed over the years was represented as in-universe organizational change and it was really a delight to read, and to top it all off you ended an article in 2018 with an assignment to D-class Keter Duty in a way that was both believable and satisfying.

Just incredibly well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you — that GoI-ball is adorable xD

Re: SRAs as anomalous: It fits more into my own head-canon that parascience is anomalous; technology built on something that can't always be trusted to behave in a predictable fashion. In my mind, that's what makes parascience parascience, rather than just — science.

I definitely presented SRAs here in a way to facilitate the story I wanted to tell, though. One of my goals was to present a solid reason they can't be used in all cases (the more we use, the more unreliable they all become — also, they just don't work on some anomalies), but my first goal was the idea of malfunctioning SRAs producing an ecological disaster. That resulted in the SRAs taking on a particular set of properties — properties that, yeah, I understand won't work for everyone.

I still kind of hope "morphogenic field" catches on as an alternative to "reality warping", though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personnally I don't really like the SRA headcanon here, because the whole, it's just like the anomalies and we don't really understand it thing feels kinda cliche… And I guess the way is works is kind of different from the how SRA commonly goes, and to me it feels more like tweaking existing framework just so that it can be dangerous here, and I guess I can't really buy into that…

But the last note (and the build up of characters for that) absolutely win me over, so upvote :D
What can I say I'm biased towards powerful Foundation and positive looks on it; but nah even aside from that it's amazing enough.

Love the O5-5 here, so please have this GOIball thing:

+ Awwww
- It has a conscience now
It's a delight to be able to upvote this to +100. What a magnificent return.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, no. I very specifically and intentionally decided not to name it that (if only to avoid confusion xD).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You missed the opportunity to name SARC the Sommerfield Containment Panel

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a creator, you do not owe us anything. If there's ever a reason why doing what you do causes you undue stress, then by all means, you should do something else.

With that said, I'd be lying if I said I wasn't ecstatic to see you back. And boy oh boy, what a return this is. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't thought of that, but yeah — it's possible that shutting an SRA down mid-operation could have catastrophic consequences, and it's better to just let the thing burn itself out. Have two other SRAs suppress its effects until it either dies quietly or someone comes in to replace it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the autoshutdown, I'll also throw in that in industrial settings the best option isn't always "immediately shut down", so instead of fail-safe you have fail-soft. Say, if coolant pump #3 is failing on some piece of processing equipment that has a shutdown time measured in days, you'd want it to set off an alarm of some sort, but you'd also want it to keep operating with whatever capacity it has left as long as it can. Better to have to replace a burned out pump than have the plant explode or lose millions in salvageable product because the pump sensed it was overheating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It happened purely by accident. I wrote his bit, and was trying to make up an example for him to bring up — then I remembered an article actually described the precise thing he was talking about. >_>

Also, "Old Man Jenkins" remains one of my favorite bits of this skip. Particularly since it's actually a direct-but-underhanded reference to another -J skip (in fact, I thought about crosslinking it, but decided that crosslinking to a -J would be over the line).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Besides being a great overall story, I really appreciate the use of cross-linking (my article, also appreciated) not as a pertinent part of the plot or a useless “we tested it on blank”, but as real world building. This dude heard about what happened in another (thematically similar) story and was able to draw the line, and that’s really cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a pretty good point — I was thinking about which to link to, and I settled on 106. But there's no reason not to link to them both that way. I'll add it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that rather than to leave the Scranton connection as a bonus gleamed in the discussion page, "old" should link to 106 and "colleague" should link to Until Death, so that last line has maximum punch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpick, I'm not really clear on why the SRA array can automatically detect a unit failure and suppress its anomalous effects, but can't divert power/turn the unit off without human assistance. Obviously you'd need to replace the faulty unit

Yeah, I'm kind of just fudging explanations there:

SRA Details/Author Canon
– hide block

The reason I imagined you can't just shut it off is complicated, and isn't touched on in the email. In my canon, when an SRA fails, it doesn't necessarily go critical; sometimes it just shuts off/stops working. But every so often, it fails in such a way that it starts an internal feedback loop (where its morphogenic field actually manages to become self-sustaining), which is what creates the risk of a criticality — now, if you shut it off, it doesn't matter, because it doesn't need power to keep going. The feedback loop has to be 'broken', which is a complicated process that requires human intervention. This is what happened in the SS Sommerfeld — DeVorn went with six (rather than fifteen or eighteen); three failed, with two of the failures becoming self-sustaining — which only left three left to suppress. The two self-sustaining failures overwhelmed the three remaining 'good' units, which resulted in a gigantic criticality event — one that's still ongoing.


I realize I didn't actually elaborate any of this in the article, though, so it's a perfectly reasonable nitpick to have. >_>

Did I read this part wrong, or did O5-5 teleport a fugitive into a holding cell over email? If so holy balls is old!foundation overpowered.

Yes, that's precisely what happened — yes, old!foundation is ridiculously overpowered — and yes, I totally imagine O5-5 as Amanda Waller. xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpick, I'm not really clear on how the SRA array can automatically detect a unit failure and make appropriate corrections, but couldn't divert power/turn the unit off without human assistance. Seems like the latter would be way easier than the former (once the faulty SRA is suppressed, of course).

Anyway this skip is amazing, full stop. I especially liked the ending; I was expecting the usual "And then the anomaly creator disappeared into thin air, the Foundation is still tracking them", but nope the dude fucked up enough to draw the eye of an O5 (who I pictured as Amanda Waller) and got his comeuppance. Also:

You're probably wondering how you suddenly ended up in a cell just now. Don't worry about that; it's not important.

Did I read this part wrong, or did O5-5 teleport a fugitive into a holding cell over email? If so holy balls is old!foundation overpowered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough! Part of the idea of this was to tell the story of an anomalous 'ecological' disaster caused by faulty safety equipment (the SRAs), but presented as an official "autopsy" of the disaster itself. I disagree with previous posters who think the anomaly is irrelevant — the anomaly is the SS Sommerfeld's malfunctioning SRAs, which are creating a whirlwind of anomalous behavior (and which get demonstrably worse when you try to use more SRAs to correct them). I don't think this story would work with another anomaly in its place; the lack of focus on the anomalies themselves is only because no one can get close enough to directly observe them.

I don't think this would have worked as a tale — not for me, anyway. I wanted to write this as an SCP article, because I wanted it to be both an anomaly and an autopsy of all the fuck-ups surrounding that anomaly. To me, this article is the Foundation airing out its old laundry and trying to break down how the hell they let something like this happen.

I definitely understand if it doesn't fit for you as an SCP article, though — and I also know what you mean about the dialogue falling into that trap of becoming too "samey" in the pursuit of sounding naturalistic (that's something that I actually kept getting worried about, and led me to clean up a lot of it in later drafts >_>).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would have been wonderful as a tale. Your dialogue is excellent (though I think it can fall a bit into the Brian Michael Bendis trap of "natural" dialogue just making every character sound the same), as people have already said, and the narrative you craft is a compelling one. As a mainlist article I just don't think it works. This is a good example of how the SCP format can work against a narrative. All the most interesting parts of the article- the dialogue, the characters, the story of the Foundation trying to deal with a rogue company and technology- have to be shoved in to fit the format and it feels like it does them a disservice. Most of the characterization is done through very tell-y dialogue, we never get a sense of really knowing the characters (which is frustrating, because what I see is interesting enough to want more) and their personalities get mostly reduced to just a few lines of descriptive text each. The tension that could be achieved with a lot of the later scenes of approaching the ship and inter-character conflict is almost completely destroyed because we only see it as back-narrated after the events. As people have already said, the anomaly itself is 100% irrelevant to the narrative, which is a huge turn-off as a reader. If the anomaly doesn't matter, why is such a huge chunk of the narrative set-up devoted to describing it? Why is the story framed in a format that by its nature places the anomaly front and center to the reader? The story is too good to downvote, but I also can't upvote an article that's so clearly working against itself.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I've been tweaking this thing way more than I ought to, but I think I'm done now. If you read it when it first got published, there's only two parts I've really heavily changed:

  • The final email from DeVorn has been swapped out for one from his employees (since DeVorn was sounding a bit like a two-bit super-villain, and this gave me a chance to provide a detailed depiction of what's going wrong with the SRAs)
  • O5-5's email underwent an overhaul. It is now at least 75% more grandmotherly.

Other than that, all I did was try to streamline the description a little to make the anomaly's effect more clear, and add a bit about DeVorn being a PoI that's still at large.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't understand jackshit about the anomaly, but the story and characters are so compelling, I upvoted anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really cannot express how amazing the dialogue is. It feels natural, and with the minimum amount of actual prose you made each voice seem distinct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like turning SCPs into MacGuffins within their own articles is not a type of writing we should be striving for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once again, I have read an example of the good storytelling that this site can bring.

Good to have you back Hippo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't worry, I'm not going to get tired of hearing things like that any time soon xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great stuff, as always - I'll sound like a broken record to you, given how often I see this on your article discussion pages, but your dialogue is masterful. And I'm glad to hear you're back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting read, reminiscent of some of my favorate skips. Easy +1. This is seriously reminding me of something, however. Like one of my old favorate authors. But, no, it couldn't be. Is it…

HOLY SHIT HES BACK

Praise Hippo Jesus, he has returned! Good to have you back!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! And that's fair, yeah; part of my concern with writing this was how I was hammering out details about how Scranton Reality Anchors work and how the Foundation might deploy them.

Part of what I wanted to imply in the end is that they absolutely don't work against some anomalies; try to use something like this against 106 and he'll just laugh as he melts your face. An earlier draft had Professor Sivori go into this a bit more (mentioning how they don't do anything against certain types of anomalies), but I cut it because I was worried the middle was starting to drag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can always feel free to leave critiques on mainsite work as well!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oops — thanks for pointing that out; I missed it during a rewrite. Fixed!

Also, thanks — this was mostly inspired by 1730, but I also wanted to evoke a lot of the world-building that goes on in skips like SCP-2480.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welcome back to the site.
Please accept this duffel bag of loose upvotes, as the rating module will only let me enter one of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's for this reason precisely that I'm gonna leave my vote at neutral instead of positive.
